stem:
You are a psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old IT consultant referred by his GP for assessment of possible adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. He reports lifelong difficulty completing tasks, frequent careless mistakes, and being easily distracted. He has held his current job 8 years but says he relies on tight deadlines and a self-built reminder system. He achieved high school graduation with average results despite his difficulties.

He drinks 2 standard drinks per week and uses no other substances. His mood is euthymic. He has no anxiety or sleep disturbance. His mother reports he was always restless in primary school but managed academically.

data_table.Examination: Mental state normal, no thought disorder, no psychomotor abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Standardised adult ADHD rating scale: 18 of 18 inattentive items endorsed at threshold severity, 6 of 9 hyperactive-impulsive items, symptoms present since age 7, impairment present in occupational and home domains

lead_in: Which feature is essential to support an adult ADHD diagnosis under the Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline?

options.text: Documented IQ below 90 to establish baseline cognitive impairment before stimulant therapy
options.rationale: ADHD is diagnosed regardless of IQ. Many adults with ADHD have above-average IQ and have compensated for impairment until adult demands exceed their capacity. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: At least 6 of 9 symptoms in BOTH inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive domains for adult diagnosis
options.rationale: DSM-5 lowers the adult threshold to 5 of 9 in either domain (not both). The combined-presentation requirement of 6 of 9 in both applies only to children under 17. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Persistent functional impairment in two or more settings attributable to symptoms present before 12 years old
options.rationale: The Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD aligns with DSM-5 in requiring symptoms before age 12, present in two or more settings, with clear functional impairment. Without childhood onset and cross-setting impairment, an adult presentation of inattention is more likely explained by other conditions. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Normal brain MRI and electroencephalogram excluding structural and epileptiform causes
options.rationale: Routine MRI and EEG are not part of the standard ADHD workup. They are reserved for atypical features such as focal neurology or possible seizures. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Failed trial of nonpharmacological strategies including cognitive behavioural therapy and coaching
options.rationale: Behavioural and educational strategies can be used in parallel with or before pharmacotherapy but are not a diagnostic prerequisite. The diagnosis is based on history, examination, and ratings, not therapy response. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis under the Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline requires DSM-5 criteria: at least 5 of 9 inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (lower threshold than child criteria of 6), symptoms before age 12, present in 2 or more settings, with clear functional impairment, not better explained by another disorder. Collateral history (school reports, parent interview) anchors the childhood onset criterion. Stimulant treatment in adults requires ECG only if cardiovascular risk factors or symptoms, BP/HR monitoring before and during treatment, and substance use assessment.
key_points: Adult DSM-5 threshold: 5 of 9 symptoms in either domain (vs 6 of 9 in children)
Symptoms must be present before age 12 with cross-setting impairment
Routine MRI and EEG not required
Stimulants require BP/HR monitoring; ECG only if cardiovascular risk
explanation.pearls: Roughly 60 to 70% of adults with ADHD have at least one comorbid psychiatric condition (depression, anxiety, substance use, or personality disorder), and untreated comorbidities significantly worsen functional outcomes.

sources.label: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar asked to assess a 68-year-old woman admitted to a Victorian inpatient unit with severe depression with psychotic features. She has lost 11 kg over 3 months, refuses food and fluids, has expressed delusional guilt about being responsible for a recent natural disaster, and has nihilistic delusions. Three antidepressant trials and augmentation have failed over 6 months.

She is now drowsy from dehydration and at imminent risk of physical deterioration. The treating consultant has recommended electroconvulsive therapy. She declines treatment, stating it will erase her family from her memory and she deserves to die. There is no advance care directive. Family supports treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, Temp 36.6 degrees C, RR 18, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Marked psychomotor retardation, food refusal, mucous membranes dry, MMSE 22 of 30 limited by lack of engagement
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 132 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, ECG sinus tachycardia, no QT prolongation

lead_in: Under the Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act, what is the most appropriate next step regarding ECT?

options.text: Commence ECT today under emergency provisions without tribunal review given her immediate risk
options.rationale: Victorian law does not allow non-emergency ECT without tribunal authorisation for non-consenting patients. Emergency provisions are limited and specific. The clinical situation, while serious, allows time to apply to the tribunal. (Source: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022)
source_refs: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

options.text: Respect her refusal and continue antidepressant escalation with intravenous hydration as supportive care
options.rationale: Three antidepressant trials have failed and she is at imminent risk of death from food and fluid refusal. Continuing failed medication strategies without addressing her acute psychotic depression is not in keeping with least-restrictive treatment when ECT could rapidly resolve the depression. (Source: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022)
source_refs: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

options.text: Obtain consent from her next of kin and proceed with ECT under that family authorisation alone
options.rationale: Family consent does not substitute for tribunal authorisation in Victoria. Next of kin can provide views but cannot consent to ECT for an adult patient who lacks capacity. (Source: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022)
source_refs: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

options.text: Apply to the Mental Health Tribunal for an ECT order as she lacks capacity and treatment is the least restrictive option
options.rationale: Under the Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022, ECT for a person who is unable to give informed consent and who is a compulsory or security patient requires an order from the Mental Health Tribunal. Her psychotic depression with nihilistic delusions, food refusal, and physical deterioration meets criteria for compulsory treatment and her refusal is driven by psychotic illness rather than informed weighing of risks. (Source: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022)
source_refs: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

options.text: Transfer to the medical ward for nasogastric feeding and defer mental health treatment until capacity recovers
options.rationale: Nasogastric feeding alone treats the dehydration but not the psychotic depression driving her food refusal. Definitive psychiatric treatment is required and ECT can be sought concurrently with tribunal authorisation. (Source: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022)
source_refs: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

explanation.summary: Electroconvulsive therapy in Australia is governed by state mental health legislation. In Victoria the Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022 requires that ECT for a compulsory patient who cannot give informed consent be authorised by the Mental Health Tribunal. Capacity is decision-specific: psychotic depression with nihilistic delusions typically impairs the patient's ability to weigh information about ECT. ECT response rates in severe psychotic depression are approximately 80 to 90%, faster than any pharmacotherapy. Cognitive side effects (transient anterograde amnesia) are real but typically resolve within weeks.
key_points: ECT in Victoria for a non-consenting compulsory patient requires Mental Health Tribunal order
Capacity is decision-specific and impaired by psychotic depression in many cases
ECT remission rates in severe psychotic depression: 80 to 90%
Most cognitive side effects resolve within 2 to 6 weeks; some persistent autobiographical gaps in a minority
explanation.pearls: Psychotic depression with food refusal and rapid physical decline has a higher response rate to ECT (80 to 90%) than to combination antidepressant and antipsychotic pharmacotherapy (around 60%), and ECT remits symptoms in around 2 to 4 weeks rather than 6 to 8.

sources.label: Victorian Mental Health and Wellbeing Act 2022

stem: You are an infectious diseases registrar reviewing a 34-year-old man from a high-prevalence country who arrived in Australia 18 months ago. He is screened before commencing infliximab for severe rheumatoid arthritis. He is asymptomatic, with no cough, fever, or weight loss. His QuantiFERON-Plus assay is positive. Chest X-ray is normal. There are no apical changes or calcified granulomas. He drinks no alcohol, has no past liver disease, and his ALT and AST are normal. He has no household contacts known to have active tuberculosis. He is keen to start his rheumatology treatment as soon as possible.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 124/76 mmHg, Temp 36.7 degrees C, BMI 25 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Cardiorespiratory examination unremarkable, mild synovitis of the MCP joints
data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive, CXR normal, ALT 22 U/L, AST 24 U/L, HIV non-reactive, HepBsAg negative, HepC negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for his latent tuberculosis infection?

options.text: Sputum induction for acid-fast bacilli smear and culture before any latent treatment is started
options.rationale: He has no respiratory symptoms and a normal chest X-ray, so sputum sampling is not required. Active TB workup is reserved for symptomatic or radiographically suspicious cases. (Source: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection)
source_refs: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

options.text: Defer treatment and commence infliximab immediately given normal chest imaging and no symptoms
options.rationale: Anti-TNF therapy in the presence of untreated latent TB carries a 4 to 8-fold increase in reactivation, often as extrapulmonary or miliary disease. Treatment of latent TB must precede or run alongside biologic therapy. (Source: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection)
source_refs: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

options.text: Four-drug RIPE therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for 2 months then dual therapy for 4 months
options.rationale: RIPE is the regimen for active tuberculosis, not latent infection. Treating latent infection as if it were active causes unnecessary toxicity. (Source: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection)
source_refs: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular BCG vaccination as protective immunisation prior to biologic therapy
options.rationale: BCG vaccination does not treat existing latent infection and is contraindicated in immunosuppressed adults due to disseminated BCG risk. (Source: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection)
source_refs: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg orally daily with pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 6 months and monthly clinical and LFT review
options.rationale: Isoniazid 300 mg daily plus pyridoxine for 6 months is a recommended Australian regimen for latent tuberculosis. Treatment should start at least 4 weeks before commencing anti-TNF therapy to reduce the risk of reactivation. Pyridoxine prevents peripheral neuropathy. (Source: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection)
source_refs: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

explanation.summary: Latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) treatment is offered to those at risk of progression: recent contacts of infectious cases, immunosuppression including anti-TNF therapy, HIV, dialysis, organ transplant, silicosis, and migrants from high-prevalence countries. Acceptable Australian regimens include isoniazid 6 months, isoniazid plus rifampicin 3 months, and rifampicin 4 months. Anti-TNF agents increase reactivation risk 4 to 8-fold; LTBI treatment should begin at least 4 weeks before biologic therapy. Pyridoxine prevents isoniazid peripheral neuropathy.
key_points: Recommended LTBI regimens: 6H, 3HR, or 4R as per NTAC
Anti-TNF therapy increases TB reactivation 4 to 8-fold
LTBI treatment should start at least 4 weeks before biologic therapy
Pyridoxine prevents isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy
explanation.pearls: Isoniazid hepatotoxicity rises markedly above age 35 and with alcohol intake - baseline ALT and monthly LFT monitoring during treatment catches early hepatitis and most resolves with prompt drug cessation.

sources.label: NTAC - Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent Tuberculosis Infection

stem:
You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 56-year-old man at a 3-month follow-up after an unprovoked proximal deep vein thrombosis of the right popliteal vein. He has been treated with apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for 3 months and is well. He has had no further symptoms. He has no personal or family history of VTE.

He has no history of cancer, has lost no weight, and a malignancy screen at presentation was unremarkable. There is no thrombophilia identified on a basic screen. His mild hypertension is well controlled. He has no bleeding history.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, BMI 27 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: No leg swelling, no calf tenderness, no signs of post-thrombotic syndrome, no bruising
data_table.Investigations: Hb 142 g/L, platelets 235 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73m2, ALT 24 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing anticoagulation plan?

options.text: Switch to warfarin with target INR 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely as the most cost-effective long-term option
options.rationale: DOACs are preferred over warfarin for VTE in patients without contraindications because they offer similar efficacy with lower intracranial bleeding rates and avoid INR monitoring. Switching for cost alone is not recommended. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Stop apixaban at 3 months given the patient has completed standard treatment and is asymptomatic
options.rationale: Stopping anticoagulation after 3 months for unprovoked proximal DVT leaves a recurrence risk of around 10% in the first year, with case fatality of recurrent VTE around 5%. Extended therapy is recommended in low-bleeding-risk patients. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Switch to aspirin 100 mg daily as long-term secondary prevention with no further anticoagulant
options.rationale: Aspirin reduces VTE recurrence by only around 30% compared with around 80% reduction from a DOAC, with similar bleeding rates at the low-dose DOAC extended dose. DOAC is preferred. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Continue apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely without reducing the dose for extended therapy
options.rationale: Both AMPLIFY-EXT and EINSTEIN-CHOICE showed that reduced-dose DOACs are as effective and safer for extended-phase therapy after the initial 6 months. Full-dose is reserved for higher-risk profiles. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Continue apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily indefinitely with annual bleeding-risk review and renal monitoring
options.rationale: After an unprovoked proximal DVT, indefinite anticoagulation is recommended for low bleeding-risk patients because annual recurrence after stopping is around 10% in year one and 5% per year thereafter. Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily is the evidence-supported extended-phase dose with similar efficacy and lower bleeding than full-intensity therapy. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

explanation.summary: VTE anticoagulation duration depends on whether the event was provoked or unprovoked and on bleeding risk. Provoked VTE (surgery, trauma, immobilisation) is treated for 3 months. Unprovoked proximal DVT or PE warrants extended (indefinite) anticoagulation in low-bleeding-risk patients because recurrence is around 10% in year one without therapy. Reduced-dose DOACs (apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily) are as effective and safer than full dose for the extended phase. Annual reassessment of bleeding risk is essential.
key_points: Provoked VTE: 3 months of anticoagulation
Unprovoked proximal VTE: extended therapy if low bleeding risk
Reduced-dose DOAC (apixaban 2.5 mg BID, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily) for extended phase
Annual bleeding-risk reassessment is the cornerstone of extended therapy
explanation.pearls: After the first 6 months of full-dose anticoagulation for unprovoked VTE, reduced-dose apixaban (2.5 mg twice daily) lowers recurrence by approximately 80% compared with placebo with major bleeding rates similar to no anticoagulation.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand

stem:
You are the medical registrar at a metropolitan emergency department. A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 3 days of dysuria, rigors, and now confusion. He has been brought in by his wife. He is alert but confused, peripherally cool, and tachypnoeic. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. He weighs 80 kg. He has no known allergies. He has no known kidney disease.

Intravenous access has been established. A urinalysis is positive for nitrites and leucocytes. A point-of-care lactate has been performed and blood cultures sent. He has received no antibiotics today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124 bpm, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 28, Temp 39.4 degrees C, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 13, mottled lower limbs, suprapubic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 12 mmol/L, creatinine 158 micromol/L, WCC 18 x10^9/L, CRP 220 mg/L, urinalysis nitrites and leucocytes positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate first-hour intervention?

options.text: Commence intravenous fluid resuscitation at 1 L over 30 minutes then reassess before further bolus
options.rationale: 1 L is well below the recommended 30 mL/kg initial volume for septic shock and inadequate for this 80 kg patient. The Surviving Sepsis bundle calls for 30 mL/kg in the first 3 hours, ideally front-loaded in the first hour. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network

options.text: Defer antibiotics until urine culture and blood culture results are available to allow targeted therapy
options.rationale: Each hour of delayed antibiotic administration in septic shock increases mortality by 4 to 8%. Cultures are obtained but treatment is not delayed. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network

options.text: Administer intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g and commence 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid resuscitation
options.rationale: Septic shock with hypotension and lactate over 2 mmol/L mandates broad-spectrum antibiotics within the first hour and 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid resuscitation. Piperacillin-tazobactam covers community urinary sepsis with shock per Australian sepsis pathways. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion via peripheral cannula without fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is added when fluid resuscitation has failed to restore MAP above 65 mmHg. Starting vasopressors before fluid in this patient bypasses the proven mortality-reducing fluid bolus. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg as first-line therapy for hypotension before fluids
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is added only when fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support have failed to maintain MAP above 65 mmHg. It is not a first-hour intervention before fluids and antibiotics. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network)
source_refs: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis hour-1 bundle for adults with septic shock includes obtaining lactate and blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L, and vasopressor support if MAP remains below 65 mmHg after fluid resuscitation. Empirical antibiotic choice follows source: urinary tract sepsis with shock is commonly treated with piperacillin-tazobactam in Australia, narrowed when cultures return. Source control (drainage, surgical intervention) is pursued in parallel for abscess, biliary sepsis, or other surgical foci.
key_points: Antibiotics within 1 hour of septic shock recognition
30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for shock or lactate > 4 mmol/L
Noradrenaline first-line vasopressor target MAP > 65 mmHg
Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day if vasopressor-dependent shock persists
explanation.pearls: Each hour of delay in appropriate antibiotic administration after recognition of septic shock increases in-hospital mortality by approximately 4 to 8% - early administration is among the highest-yield interventions in sepsis care.

sources.label: Surviving Sepsis Campaign and Australian Sepsis Network
ANZICS Clinical Trials Group - ANZICS Sepsis Resources
